title: NATO 5% Rearmament Meets PAC-3/THAAD Production Framework Agreements
summary: Lockheed signed 7-year framework deals to triple PAC-3 and quadruple THAAD output, with NATO's 5% GDP commitment locking in multi-decade demand — LMT's 21% pullback prices in F-35/F-16 delays while ignoring the missile ramp.
catalyst: On January 6, 2026 Lockheed Martin and the U.S. Department of War signed a 7-year framework agreement to scale PAC-3 MSE interceptor production from ~600 to 2,000 units annually, followed January 29, 2026 by a parallel agreement to quadruple THAAD output from 96 to 400 interceptors per year [1][2]. On May 21, 2026 Lockheed broke ground on a new Munitions Production Center in Troy, Alabama — part of a $9 billion munitions investment through 2030 [3]. These framework agreements arrived after NATO's June 2025 Hague Summit commitment that all Allies invest 5% of GDP on defense by 2035 (3.5% core + 1.5% related), an unprecedented multi-decade demand backstop [4].
thesis: The framework-agreement model — long-term, fixed-rate volume commitments enabling supplier capex — structurally re-rates the missile defense businesses at LMT and RTX from cyclical earners to utility-like compounders backed by congressional appropriations and allied commitments. PAC-3 MSE and Raytheon's PAC-2 GEM-T are the only combat-proven, currently deployed Patriot-family interceptors on Earth, and every additional German, Dutch, Polish, and Romanian Patriot battery sold creates a 30-year aftermarket annuity. The market is punishing LMT's Aeronautics segment (F-16/C-130 delays) without crediting the Missiles & Fire Control segment that is now under contracted 3-4x production-rate increases.
body_md:
## Setup

The defense investment cycle in 2026 is fundamentally different from any previous one in the post-Cold War era. Three structural shifts arrived inside six months and the equity market has not finished pricing them.

First, on **June 24–25, 2025**, NATO leaders at The Hague summit agreed to a binding commitment that all Allies — with Spain as a single carve-out — invest **5% of GDP on defense by 2035**, split between 3.5% core defense expenditure and 1.5% defense-and-security-related spending [4]. Allies must submit annual incremental plans, with a formal review in 2029. This is not a press release — it is a treaty-level commitment that anchors a 10-year procurement plan.

Second, on **January 6, 2026**, Lockheed Martin and the U.S. Department of War signed a **7-year framework agreement** to lift PAC-3 MSE interceptor output from approximately 600 units per year to **2,000 units per year** — a more than threefold increase, anchored by long-term volume certainty enabling supplier capex [1]. Lockheed CEO Jim Taiclet: "We will create unprecedented capacity for PAC-3 MSE production, delivering at the speed our nation and allies demand." Three weeks later, on **January 29, 2026**, the parties signed a parallel framework agreement to **quadruple THAAD interceptor production from 96 to 400 per year** over the same 7-year window [2].

Third, on **May 21, 2026** — two days ago as of this writing — Lockheed broke ground on a new Munitions Production Center (Building 47) in **Troy, Alabama**, adding 87,000 square feet and nearly doubling the site's THAAD/Next-Generation Interceptor production footprint, part of a **$9 billion munitions investment through 2030** [3]. Lockheed now operates 340,000+ sq ft of THAAD production across 9 US sites, supported by 750 suppliers in 42 states.

## Why Now

Germany's 2026 defense budget is **€117.2B, up from €95B in 2025** [5]. France's 2026 allocation is **€68.5B (2.25% of GDP)**, the Netherlands has more than doubled its budget since 2021 to **€25.8B**, and Northern European allies (Poland, Baltic states) are already above 3% [5]. None of these countries can wait until 2035 — Russian missile threats against Ukraine and probing flights into the Baltics have collapsed political resistance to spending. The CSIS-tracked European "ASAP" for air defense framework is creating a buyer's queue, not a buyer's market.

At the same time, the supply curve is fixed for ~24 months. Raytheon's PAC-2 GEM-T program has a contracted backlog of **~1,500 missiles and near-term demand for another 1,000**, while production is just **20 missiles/month** today and will reach only **35/month by end-2027** [6]. The 1,000-unit NATO Support and Procurement Agency PAC-2 order — including a dedicated German tranche — has already enabled a Raytheon-MBDA joint venture in Germany [6]. Anyone wanting a Patriot system in 2026 is in a queue.

## Company-Specific Edge — LMT

Lockheed's Missiles & Fire Control (M&FC) segment grew **+8% in Q1 2026 to $3.65B** as PAC-3, JASSM, LRASM and PrSM ramped [7]. In that single quarter the segment booked **~$7 billion in PAC-3 orders, including a $4.7 billion undefinitized contract** for accelerated production — meaning LMT can begin spending against the order before final terms close [7]. Backlog ended Q1 at **$186.4B**, with management noting more than half converts to revenue within 24 months [7]. Yet **LMT trades at $533 vs. its March 2, 2026 all-time high of $676 — a 21% drawdown** triggered by F-16 and C-130 schedule slips and a Q1 earnings miss, with Citi cutting its target from $675 to $571 on May 18, 2026 [8].

The screener report for LMT shows **forward P/E of 19.15, ROE of 76.87%, EPS growth +161% YoY, and AI Resilience score of 8/10** (defense is fundamentally AI-enabled, not AI-disrupted). At a $533 price the market is paying ~20x for a business with a $194B record backlog, $9B in committed munitions capex, and 7-year contracted volume ramps in two flagship programs. The Aeronautics overhang creates the entry.

## Company-Specific Edge — RTX

RTX closed Q1 2026 with a **$271 billion total backlog, up 25% YoY**, and a quarterly book-to-bill of 1.14 [9]. The Raytheon (defense) segment alone delivered **$6.9B in sales (+10%)** driven by Patriot/GEM-T and naval munitions, booking **$6.6B in awards** that included a **$600M Netherlands Patriot equipment award** and **$400M from the U.S. Army for missile defense sensors** [9]. Raytheon's rolling-12-month book-to-bill is **1.48 — well above the 1.0 line that signals revenue acceleration**. RTX guided 2026 adjusted EPS to **$6.60–$6.80** and raised the figure with Q1 [9]. Munitions output across RTX rose **+40% YoY in Q1** [10].

The screener report for RTX flags forward P/E of 25.18, AI Resilience 8/10, profit margin 7.6%, and revenue growth 12.1% YoY. The premium multiple is earned — RTX's $1B+ Patriot supply chain investment is converting backlog into deliveries faster than any other prime [6].

## Numbers That Matter

| Metric | LMT | RTX |
|---|---|---|
| Stock price (May 22, 2026) | $533 [8] | $177 [11] |
| Forward P/E | 19.15 (screener) | 25.18 (screener) |
| Total backlog | $186.4B Q1'26 [7] | $271B Q1'26 [9] |
| Backlog growth YoY | flat (~5%) | +25% [9] |
| Key segment Q1 growth | M&FC +8% [7] | Raytheon +10% [9] |
| Production framework | PAC-3 600→2,000/yr; THAAD 96→400/yr [1][2] | PAC-2 GEM-T 20→35/month [6] |
| Munitions capex | $9B through 2030 [3] | $1B+ Patriot supply chain [6] |
| Recent allied order | $4.7B PAC-3 undefinitized [7] | $600M Netherlands Patriot [9] |

## Bear Case

Three real risks. (1) The framework agreements still require **annual congressional appropriations** to fund — a continuing resolution or partial shutdown could slip the ramp by a quarter or two. (2) **Allied free-riding**: Spain has already opted out of the 5% commitment, and a populist election in Germany or France could push the 3.5% core target out beyond 2035. (3) **Aeronautics earnings drag at LMT**: if F-35 Block 4/TR-3 delays cascade or C-130 deliveries slip further, the missile-segment story gets buried for another two quarters — the May 18 Citi downgrade to $571 reflects exactly this concern [8]. The cleanest version of this trade is RTX, which has less aeronautics commercial exposure to drag the narrative.

## Conclusion

The framework-agreement structure announced January 2026 is not a one-off contract — it is a **new procurement model** that converts US missile-defense primes from cyclical contractors into utility-like compounders. NATO's 5% GDP commitment, signed at The Hague nine months earlier, creates 30+ allied buyers competing for the same fixed production slots. Lockheed has now broken ground on the physical capacity to deliver. The setup is asymmetric because the equity market is anchoring on LMT's Aeronautics noise and RTX's commercial aerospace lag, while the missile-defense capacity expansion — supported by $10B+ in combined capex and 7-year volume contracts — is the durable story.

ticker_rationale.LMT: Forward P/E of 19.15 (screener) with a record $194B FY-end 2025 backlog, $7B PAC-3 orders booked in Q1 2026 alone, and 7-year framework agreements to scale PAC-3 from 600 to 2,000/yr and THAAD from 96 to 400/yr — yet trading 21% below its March 2026 ATH on F-16/C-130 noise [7][8].
ticker_rationale.RTX: Q1 2026 backlog of $271B (+25% YoY) with Raytheon book-to-bill of 1.48 rolling-12 and munitions output +40% YoY; $1B+ Patriot supply-chain investment is the bottleneck-breaker for the 1,500-unit PAC-2 GEM-T backlog [6][9].

risks: Congressional continuing resolution or shutdown in FY26 appropriations delays the initial PAC-3/THAAD framework contract awards and pushes ramp into 2027
Further F-35 Block 4 / TR-3 software delays or C-130 schedule slips at LMT Aeronautics extend the segment overhang and bury the M&FC story for 2–3 more quarters
Spain-style opt-outs spread among NATO members (e.g., post-election Germany or France), softening the 3.5% core defense floor and slowing European Patriot/THAAD orders
Iran-US de-escalation reduces near-term political urgency for European air defense restocking, even if structural NATO commitment holds
invalidation: LMT's Missiles & Fire Control segment revenue growth decelerates below +5% YoY for two consecutive quarters, indicating the framework ramp is stalling
RTX's Raytheon segment book-to-bill drops below 1.2 on a rolling-12-month basis, signaling allied order flow has peaked

Congressional FY26 defense appropriations bill is signed and the initial PAC-3 and THAAD framework contract awards are below $4B combined (vs. the $4.7B undefinitized PAC-3 Q1 booking already in hand)
LMT stock closes below $475 on a confirmed F-35 production halt or C-130 program write-down (the original Aeronautics overhang materializes worse than feared)
